note: CDC-style cold-chain guidance snippets, PARAPHRASED for this demo from public vaccine storage-and-handling practice. Not verbatim CDC text; cite the real CDC Vaccine Storage & Handling Toolkit in production.

snippets.title: Refrigerated vaccines must be kept at 2-8 C
snippets.text: Store refrigerated vaccines between 2 C and 8 C (36-46 F) with a target of 5 C. Any reading outside this band is a temperature excursion and must be documented and assessed before affected doses are used.

snippets.title: Excursions beyond 15 minutes require assessment
snippets.text: A temperature excursion above 8 C sustained for more than 15 minutes requires immediate action: label the vaccines DO NOT USE, keep them refrigerated, document the duration and peak temperature, and contact the manufacturer or state program before administering any dose.

snippets.source: CDC-style Storage & Handling (paraphrase), Storage Unit Care
snippets.title: Door left ajar is a leading excursion cause
snippets.text: A door left ajar or a worn door gasket is one of the most common causes of vaccine temperature excursions. Check that the door closes and seals automatically, use door alarms where available, and never store vaccines in the door shelves.

snippets.title: Automatic defrost cycles cause brief benign spikes
snippets.text: Household-grade and purpose-built units with automatic defrost produce short, recurring cabinet-temperature rises during the defrost cycle. Brief, bounded, periodic spikes that return to setpoint are expected equipment behavior and are not by themselves a reportable excursion, but they must remain within documented limits.

snippets.title: Power failure protocol
snippets.text: During a power failure keep storage unit doors closed, record the outage start and end, and record cabinet temperature as soon as power returns. Vaccines exposed during an outage must be marked DO NOT USE until viability is confirmed; do not discard doses before assessment by the manufacturer or immunization program.

snippets.title: Do not discard vaccines before assessment
snippets.text: Never discard vaccines after an excursion until the event has been assessed. Quarantine the doses at proper temperature, document the event, and obtain a viability determination from the manufacturer or the immunization program.

snippets.title: Scheduled maintenance and aging compressors
snippets.text: Storage units require routine preventive maintenance: clean coils, inspect door gaskets, and service compressors showing degraded performance such as a slowly rising average cabinet temperature or longer duty cycles. A drifting daily mean temperature with an intact defrost pattern suggests failing cooling capacity and warrants service before the unit exits the 2-8 C band.

snippets.title: Continuous temperature monitoring and record retention
snippets.text: Use a continuous temperature monitoring device with the probe placed centrally among the vaccines. Review and record temperatures at least twice each workday, retain logs for at least three years, and investigate every out-of-range reading.

snippets.title: Program compliance grading
snippets.text: Program participation requires documented, continuous cold-chain compliance. Repeated undocumented excursions or missing temperature logs downgrade a provider's compliance standing and can suspend vaccine allocation until corrective action is verified.

snippets.title: Water bottles add thermal mass and slow excursions
snippets.text: Keep water bottles in the refrigerator door and empty spaces. The added thermal mass slows cabinet warming during door openings and power failures, extending the time before stored vaccines reach unsafe temperatures.
